phrases.text_local: Allez les Wildcats ! On va gagner !
phrases.pronunciation: ah-LAY lay WILD-cats ! on va gah-NYAY !
phrases.cultural_note: French fans cheer with 'Allez !' the way English speakers say 'Go!' — it literally means 'Go on!' and is used for any sport.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for directions to my seats
phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, pouvez-vous m'indiquer où se trouvent les places section vingt-deux ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-KYOO-zay mwah, poo-VAY voo man-dee-KAY oo se troov lay PLASS sek-SYON van-duh ?
phrases.cultural_note: Always open with 'Excusez-moi' when asking a stranger for help — skipping it can seem abrupt in French culture.

responses.french: Oui, c'est par là, au deuxième niveau.
responses.english: Yes, it's that way, on the second level.
responses.phonetic: WEE, say par LAH, oh duh-ZYEM nee-VOH.

responses.french: Continuez tout droit et prenez l'escalier à droite.
responses.english: Keep going straight and take the stairway on the right.
responses.phonetic: con-tee-NYOO-ay too DRWAH ay pruh-NAY less-kah-LYAY ah DRWAHT.

phrases.intent_en: Order food at the concession stand
phrases.text_local: Je voudrais un hot-dog et une bière, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh voo-DRAY un HOT-dog ay oon BYAIR, seel voo PLAY.
phrases.cultural_note: The phrase 's'il vous plaît' (please) is essential — using it instantly marks you as polite to any French speaker.

responses.french: Bien sûr ! Ça fait huit dollars cinquante.
responses.english: Of course! That'll be eight dollars fifty.
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR ! sah fay WEET dol-AR san-KANT.

responses.french: Vous voulez de la moutarde ou du ketchup ?
responses.english: Would you like mustard or ketchup?
responses.phonetic: voo voo-LAY duh lah moo-TARD oo doo KET-chup ?

phrases.text_local: C'est quoi le score en ce moment ?
phrases.pronunciation: say KWAH luh SKOR ahn suh moh-MAHN ?
phrases.cultural_note: American football is still fairly niche in France, so explaining 'le football américain' versus 'le foot' (soccer) is always a fun conversation starter.

responses.french: On mène vingt et un à quatorze !

phrases.text_local: Pardon, où sont les toilettes, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: par-DOHN, oo sohn lay twah-LET, seel voo PLAY ?
phrases.cultural_note: 'Les toilettes' is the standard term in French — saying 'la salle de bains' (bathroom) for a public restroom sounds odd, as it refers to a room with a bathtub.

responses.french: C'est au bout du couloir, à gauche.
responses.english: It's at the end of the hallway, on the left.
responses.phonetic: say oh BOO doo koo-LWAHR, ah GOHSH.

responses.french: Descendez les escaliers, c'est en bas.
responses.english: Go down the stairs, it's down below.
responses.phonetic: day-sahn-DAY lay zess-kah-LYAY, say ahn BAH.

phrases.cultural_note: In French, the English word 'touchdown' is borrowed as 'touché' — coincidentally the same word as the fencing term, which often gets a laugh.
